algorithmic recommendation systems and profiling may operate outside GDPRs scope, undermining free expression and selfdetermination.

paragraphs.text: I am writing to call for urgent reforms around three critical areas: 1. Stop Killing Games Recent decisions by major gaming platforms like Steam and itch.io to remove thousands of adultthemed gamesdue to restrictions imposed by payment processors such as MasterCard, Visa, and PayPalexemplify how nonplatform entities can wield disproportionate power over digital content. These sweeping removals have impacted queerthemed titles and independent creative expression, resulting in de facto censorship without legal mandate. This censorshipbyfiat bypasses democratic norms and undermines creative diversity and freedom of expression. I urge the Commission to address the regulatory influence of payment processors, which must not override legal content or systematically curtail legitimate artistic or cultural expressions. Digital Strategy 2. Stop Payment Processor Monopoly The episode above highlights how payment processors exert implicit editorial control over digital platformsoften without transparency, appeal, or regulatory oversight. The EU must mandate that payment platforms do not unilaterally determine what content remains monetizable under vague moral criteria. There must be enforceable safeguards ensuring platform and developer recourse, proportionality, and procedural transparency when payment services affect lawful content. This aligns with broader concerns about platform power, gatekeeping, and interoperability raised by civil society groups such as EFF, Panoptykon Foundation, CDT, and AlgorithmWatch. Panoptykon Foundation 3. Stop Online Censorship & Mass Surveillance While the DSA rightly mandates action against illegal content, it also introduces risks of overmoderation, automated deplatforming, and opaque algorithmic censorship. Civil society experts have highlighted how algorithmic recommendation systems and profiling may operate outside GDPRs scope, undermining free expression and selfdetermination. The proposed ability for authorities or platforms to mandate scanning or moderation carries the risk of turning everyday digital communication into surveillance. Notably, public criticism has emerged over the Commissions microtargeted political messaging campaigns (e.g. on X), which raise serious privacy and democratic legitimacy concerns. To safeguard users, the DSA review (due by November 2025) must prioritize: Algorithmic transparency (e.g. explainable content moderation and recommendations); public and individual audit rights; real access to independent redress mechanisms and DSA-certified disputes bodies (Appeals Centre Europe, etc.). Strict prohibition of general monitoring obligations; only content flagged via court orders should trigger removal processes. Platforms must resist pressure to autofilter lawful speech and fully protect anonymity and private communications. Links and Sources: [Guardian Article: Why did thousands of adult titles just disappear from the biggest PC gaming marketplaces?]: https://shorturl.at/VVX3d [panoptykon.org: Our response in the EU consultations on the Digital Services Act]: https://shorturl.at/8FAyW [Center for Democracy & Technology: Feedback to the European Commissions Draft Proposal on the Digital Services Act] https://shorturl.at/yFenh [Out-of-court dispute settlement bodies under the Digital Services Act (DSA)]: https://shorturl.at/DJi4B
